rcomplex 0.1.0
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • Comparative co-expression analysis with rcomplex

Comparative co-expression analysis with rcomplex

Source: vignettes/rcomplex-tutorial.Rmd
rcomplex-tutorial.Rmd

This vignette demonstrates a complete rcomplex analysis using expression data from eight Pooideae grass species spanning four phylogenetic pairs, each containing one annual and one perennial species:

Species Code Life cycle Pair
Brachypodium distachyon BDIS Annual Brachypodium
Brachypodium sylvaticum BSYL Perennial Brachypodium
Hordeum vulgare HVUL Annual Hordeum
Hordeum jubatum HJUB Perennial Hordeum
Briza maxima BMAX Annual Briza
Briza media (crossmapped to BMAX) BMED Perennial Briza
Vulpia bromoides VBRO Annual Festuca/Vulpia
Festuca pratensis FPRA Perennial Festuca/Vulpia

The dataset contains the 1500 most highly expressed HOGs from VST-normalized RNA-seq from the “Live or let die?” project, stored as SummarizedExperiment objects.

1. Data preparation 

library(rcomplex)
library(SummarizedExperiment)
library(dplyr, warn.conflicts = FALSE)
library(tidyr)
library(purrr)
library(tibble)

se_list <- readRDS(system.file("extdata", "pooideae_vignette.rds",
                                package = "rcomplex"))

We organise all per-species data in a single nested tibble. Each column accumulates one processing stage — the tribble defines species metadata, then mutate + map appends the SE objects, paralog reduction, and network construction as list-columns:

Paralog reduction

Multi-copy gene families often contain recent duplicates with nearly identical expression profiles. These inflate the combinatorial search space for clique detection without adding information. reduce_orthogroups() merges correlated paralogs within each HOG using Ward.D2 hierarchical clustering on Pearson correlation distance. Paralogs above the correlation threshold (default 0.7) are replaced by their averaged expression; subfunctionalized copies with distinct expression programs are preserved:

species_data <- tribble(
  ~species, ~trait,
  "BDIS",   "annual",
  "BSYL",   "perennial",
  "HVUL",   "annual",
  "HJUB",   "perennial",
  "BMAX",   "annual",
  "BMED",   "perennial",
  "VBRO",   "annual",
  "FPRA",   "perennial"
) |>
  mutate(
    se = se_list[species],
    reduced = map(se, \(s) {
      expr <- SummarizedExperiment::assay(s, 1)
      rd <- SummarizedExperiment::rowData(s)
      ortho_sp <- tibble(gene = rownames(s),
                         hog = as.character(rd$hog)) |>
        filter(!is.na(hog))
      reduce_orthogroups(expr, ortho_sp, gene_col = "gene",
                         cor_threshold = 0.7)
    })
  )

walk2(species_data$species, species_data$reduced, \(sp, r)
  cat(sp, ":", r$n_original, "->", r$n_reduced, "genes",
      "(", r$n_merged, "merged )\n"))

Network construction

Build co-expression networks from the reduced expression matrices using Mutual Rank (MR) normalization at 3% density:

species_data <- species_data |>
  mutate(network = map(reduced, \(r)
    compute_network(r$expr_matrix, norm_method = "MR", density = 0.03)))

# Named lists for functions that expect them
species  <- species_data$species
trait_vec <- set_names(species_data$trait, species)
networks <- set_names(species_data$network, species)

Ortholog extraction

Extract ortholog pairs from HOG annotations in rowData. After paralog reduction, gene names may have changed (merged representatives), so we map through each species’ reduction via joins. Gene IDs are species-unique (genome-specific identifiers), so each species’ map is applied only to the column containing that species’ genes:

# All species pairs as a tibble
pairs <- combn(species, 2, simplify = FALSE) |>
  map(\(p) tibble(sp1 = p[1], sp2 = p[2])) |>
  list_rbind()

# Extract orthologs for every pair, then map gene names through reduction
gene_maps <- species_data |>
  transmute(species, gm = map(reduced, "gene_map")) |>
  unnest(gm) |>
  select(species, original, representative)

orthologs <- pairs |>
  mutate(data = map2(sp1, sp2, \(a, b)
    extract_orthologs(se_list[[a]], se_list[[b]]))) |>
  unnest(data) |>
  # Map Species1 genes via sp1's reduction
  left_join(gene_maps |> select(sp1 = species,
                                 Species1 = original, rep1 = representative),
            by = c("sp1", "Species1")) |>
  mutate(Species1 = coalesce(rep1, Species1)) |>
  # Map Species2 genes via sp2's reduction
  left_join(gene_maps |> select(sp2 = species,
                                 Species2 = original, rep2 = representative),
            by = c("sp2", "Species2")) |>
  mutate(Species2 = coalesce(rep2, Species2)) |>
  select(-sp1, -sp2, -rep1, -rep2) |>
  distinct()

cat(nrow(orthologs), "ortholog pairs across",
    nrow(pairs), "species pairs\n")

2. Pairwise comparison and HOG-level testing

For each of the C(8,2) = 28 species pairs, we run two analyses:

  1. Neighborhood comparison (compare_neighborhoods()): hypergeometric test for overlap of co-expression neighborhoods, producing per-pair p-values and fold-enrichment effect sizes.
  2. HOG-level permutation test (permutation_hog_test()): gene-identity permutation within each HOG. This is necessary because pair-level tests within a HOG are correlated (shared neighborhoods), making Fisher’s method or Storey q-values anti-conservative. The permutation p-value is exact regardless of the dependency structure.

With use_torch = TRUE, the fold-enrichment matrix is precomputed on GPU, reducing each permutation to fast table lookups.

The HOG-level q-values determine which edges are co-expressologs (conserved co-expression relationships):

# Helper: build edges from raw comparison + HOG-level q-values
build_perm_edges <- function(comp, hog_res, sp_a, sp_b, alpha = 0.05) {
  hog_q <- hog_res$q.value[match(comp$hog, hog_res$hog)]
  tibble(
    gene1 = comp$Species1,
    gene2 = comp$Species2,
    species1 = sp_a,
    species2 = sp_b,
    hog = comp$hog,
    q.value = hog_q,
    effect_size = sqrt(comp$Species1.effect.size * comp$Species2.effect.size),
    type = if_else(!is.na(hog_q) & hog_q < alpha, "conserved", "ns")
  )
}

n_cores <- min(4L, parallel::detectCores(logical = FALSE))

# Run all 28 pairs: nested tibble with comp + hog_res as list-columns
comparisons <- pairs |>
  mutate(
    comp = map2(sp1, sp2, \(a, b)
      compare_neighborhoods(networks[[a]], networks[[b]], orthologs)),
    hog_res = pmap(list(sp1, sp2, comp), \(a, b, c) {
      if (nrow(c) == 0) return(NULL)
      permutation_hog_test(networks[[a]], networks[[b]], c,
                           n_cores = n_cores, use_torch = TRUE)
    })
  ) |>
  filter(map_int(comp, nrow) > 0)

edges <- comparisons |>
  pmap(\(sp1, sp2, comp, hog_res, ...)
    build_perm_edges(comp, hog_res, sp1, sp2)) |>
  list_rbind()

all_hog <- comparisons |>
  transmute(hog_res = map2(hog_res, paste(sp1, sp2, sep = "-"), \(h, pair) {
    h$pair <- pair; h
  })) |>
  pull(hog_res) |>
  list_rbind()

cat(nrow(edges), "edges:", sum(edges$type == "conserved"), "conserved,",
    sum(edges$type == "ns"), "non-significant\n")
cat(nrow(all_hog), "HOG tests,",
    sum(all_hog$q.value < 0.05), "significant (q < 0.05)\n")

For quick exploration without GPU, run_pairwise_comparisons() provides a faster analytical path using pair-level Storey q-values (less statistically rigorous for multi-copy HOGs):

edges_analytical <- run_pairwise_comparisons(networks, orthologs)
cat("Analytical:", sum(edges_analytical$type == "conserved"), "conserved edges\n")
cat("Permutation:", sum(edges$type == "conserved"), "conserved edges\n")

4. Module-level analysis

Detect co-expression modules with Leiden community detection, then compare across species:

mod_bdis <- detect_modules(networks$BDIS, method = "leiden", resolution = 1.0)
mod_bsyl <- detect_modules(networks$BSYL, method = "leiden", resolution = 1.0)

mod_comp <- compare_modules(mod_bdis, mod_bsyl, orthologs,
                             method = "hypergeometric")
classes <- classify_modules(mod_comp)

show_table(as.data.frame(table(classes$classification)),
           caption = "Module classification (BDIS vs BSYL)")

Hub genes and trait conservation

Identify hub genes within each species’ modules, then classify each HOG by whether it acts as a hub across trait groups. Weighted degree (the default) is the recommended primary centrality for co-expression networks – it directly measures total co-expression strength to module neighbours, which is the standard definition of a hub gene. All three centrality measures (degree, betweenness, eigenvector) are reported regardless of which is chosen as primary:

# Comparison result for BDIS vs BSYL (for conservation tie-breaking)
comp_bdis_bsyl <- comparisons |>
  filter(sp1 == "BDIS", sp2 == "BSYL") |>
  pull(comp) |>
  pluck(1)

# Identify hubs (top 10% per module by weighted degree)
hubs_bdis <- identify_module_hubs(mod_bdis, networks$BDIS, orthologs,
                                   comparison = comp_bdis_bsyl)
hubs_bsyl <- identify_module_hubs(mod_bsyl, networks$BSYL, orthologs,
                                   comparison = comp_bdis_bsyl)

show_table(hubs_bdis |> filter(is_hub),
           caption = "Hub genes in BDIS modules")
# Classify hub conservation across traits
hub_trait <- c(BDIS = "annual", BSYL = "perennial")

hub_class <- classify_hub_conservation(
  list(BDIS = hubs_bdis, BSYL = hubs_bsyl), hub_trait,
  module_comparisons = list("BDIS.BSYL" = mod_comp)
)

show_table(hub_class |> filter(classification != "non_hub"),
           caption = "Hub conservation (BDIS vs BSYL)")

Co-expression partners of hub HOGs

After identifying trait-specific hubs, the natural question is: “Which other HOGs co-express with this hub across species?” get_coexpressed_hogs() resolves a HOG to its gene(s) in each species, extracts co-expression neighbors from the network, maps them back to HOGs, and aggregates across species. The coexpressed_traits column makes trait-group filtering straightforward:

# Pick a trait-specific hub (if any exist)
ts_hubs <- hub_class |>
  filter(classification %in% c("trait_specific_hub", "rewired_hub",
                                "conserved_hub"))

if (nrow(ts_hubs) > 0) {
  candidate <- ts_hubs$hog[1]
  cat("Querying partners of", candidate, "\n\n")

  partners <- get_coexpressed_hogs(
    candidate, networks, orthologs,
    species_trait = trait_vec, min_species = 2L,
    edges = edges
  )

  cat(nrow(partners), "partner HOGs co-expressed in >= 2 species\n")

  if (nrow(partners) > 0) {
    annual_only <- partners |>
      filter(!is.na(coexpressed_traits), coexpressed_traits == "annual")
    cat(nrow(annual_only), "annual-only partners\n")

    cross_trait <- partners |>
      filter(!is.na(coexpressed_traits),
             stringr::str_detect(coexpressed_traits, ","))
    cat(nrow(cross_trait), "cross-trait partners\n")

    show_table(head(partners, 20),
               caption = paste("Top co-expression partners of", candidate))
  }
} else {
  cat("No trait-specific hubs found in BDIS vs BSYL comparison\n")
}

5. Edge robustness: density sweep

Network density controls how many edges are retained in the co-expression network. At 3% density, only the top 3% of all pairwise MR values pass the threshold – these are the strongest co-expression relationships.

We sweep over 11 density levels centered at the 3% baseline, testing both directions:

  • Tighter (below 3%): does the signal survive stricter thresholds? Edges that persist are strong, not marginal.
  • Looser (above 3%): is the signal specific to this density, or stable across a range? Cliques that only appear at very loose thresholds are likely noise from extra edges.

The 3% choice is somewhat arbitrary. Centering the sweep around it reveals sensitivity to that choice – a truly robust co-expressolog should be present across a range of densities, not just at one particular cutoff.

The permutation pipeline is re-run at each level because network neighborhoods change with the threshold, altering the HOG-level q-values:

sweep <- tibble(multiplier = seq(0.95, 1.05, by = 0.01)) |>
  mutate(results = map(multiplier, \(m) {
    tight_nets <- map(networks, \(net)
      list(network = net$network, threshold = net$threshold * m))

    eff_density <- mean(map_dbl(networks, \(net) {
      vals <- net$network[upper.tri(net$network)]
      mean(vals >= net$threshold * m)
    }))

    tight_edges <- pairs |>
      mutate(edges = map2(sp1, sp2, \(a, b) {
        comp <- tryCatch(
          compare_neighborhoods(tight_nets[[a]], tight_nets[[b]], orthologs),
          error = \(e) NULL)
        if (is.null(comp) || nrow(comp) == 0) return(NULL)
        hog_res <- permutation_hog_test(
          tight_nets[[a]], tight_nets[[b]], comp,
          n_cores = n_cores, use_torch = TRUE)
        build_perm_edges(comp, hog_res, a, b)
      })) |>
      pull(edges) |>
      compact() |>
      list_rbind()

    if (is.null(tight_edges) || nrow(tight_edges) == 0) {
      tight_edges <- edges[0, ]
    }

    list(edges = tight_edges, eff_density = eff_density,
         n_conserved = sum(tight_edges$type == "conserved"))
  })) |>
  unnest_wider(results)

# Edge survival across density levels
sweep |>
  transmute(
    Multiplier = paste0(multiplier, "x"),
    Eff_density = sprintf("%.2f%%", eff_density * 100),
    Conserved_edges = n_conserved,
    Baseline = if_else(multiplier == 1, "***", "")
  ) |>
  show_table(caption = "Conserved edges across density levels")

The edge count may not decrease monotonically because the permutation test operates at the HOG level: tighter neighborhoods can sharpen the signal for some HOGs while eliminating it for others. Local fluctuations reflect the discrete nature of the permutation q-values. The interpretation is asymmetric: surviving tighter thresholds indicates strong signal, while surviving looser thresholds indicates specificity (not merely noise from additional edges).

Analytical alternative: clique_persistence(). The density sweep above is rigorous but expensive — it re-runs the full permutation pipeline at each threshold level. For a quick estimate, clique_persistence() computes the ratio of each co-expressolog edge’s MR value to the species’ density threshold. A persistence of 1.5 means the weakest supporting edge is 50% above threshold and would survive a 1.5x tightening. This is a principled analytical proxy: it correctly identifies the weakest link in each clique’s co-expressolog support. However, it cannot detect that tighter neighborhoods may change which HOGs reach significance in the permutation test, or that new cliques may form from HOGs that become significant at stricter thresholds. Use persistence for screening or when GPU is unavailable; use the full sweep for publication-quality robustness assessment.

6. Clique detection and phylogenetic stability

Using edges from the baseline density level, we find cliques and test their phylogenetic stability via leave-k-out jackknife. The jackknife removes k species at a time and checks whether each trait-exclusive clique is preserved:

# Use the baseline (1.0x) edges for clique detection and stability
robust_edges <- sweep |> filter(multiplier == 1) |> pull(edges) |> pluck(1)
cat("Using", sum(robust_edges$type == "conserved"), "conserved edges at",
    sprintf("%.2f%%",
            (sweep |> filter(multiplier == 1) |> pull(eff_density)) * 100),
    "density\n\n")

# Find cliques
cliques <- find_cliques(robust_edges, species, min_species = 2L)
cat(nrow(cliques), "cliques\n")

show_table(as.data.frame(table(n_species = cliques$n_species)),
           pageLength = 10, caption = "Cliques by number of species")
# Jackknife stability (all k from 1 to N-2)
max_k <- length(species) - 2L
stab <- clique_stability(robust_edges, species, trait_vec,
                          all_species = species,
                          full_cliques = cliques,
                          max_k = max_k)
cat("Trait-exclusive cliques:", length(stab$stability_class), "\n")

stab_df <- as.data.frame(table(stability_class = factor(
  stab$stability_class, levels = 0:max_k)))
show_table(stab_df, pageLength = 10, caption = "Stability class distribution")

7. HOG classification with robustness ranking

Classify each HOG by its conservation pattern, then rank within each category by robustness. The waterfall assigns each HOG to exactly one class: complete > partial > differentiated > trait_specific > unclassified.

result <- classify_cliques(robust_edges, species, trait_vec,
                            min_species = 2L,
                            stability = stab)

show_table(as.data.frame(table(classification = result$classification)),
           pageLength = 5, caption = "HOG classification summary")
  • complete: all 8 species form a clique – deeply conserved across all Pooideae
  • partial: clique spans both annual and perennial species, but not all 8
  • differentiated: annual species have a clique AND perennial species have a clique, but no cross-group conservation – lineage-specific co-expression programs
  • trait_specific: only annuals or only perennials have a clique
  • unclassified: no conservation signal detected

The robustness ranking comes from combining density sweep survival with stability class. HOGs classified at a stricter density level with high stability class are the strongest findings:

# Count how many density levels each HOG's edges survive
conserved_per_level <- sweep |>
  transmute(level = as.character(multiplier),
            hogs = map(edges, \(e) unique(e$hog[e$type == "conserved"])))

result <- result |>
  mutate(n_densities_survived = map_int(hog, \(h)
    sum(map_lgl(conserved_per_level$hogs, \(hogs) h %in% hogs))))

# Annotate trait composition from the best clique per HOG
hog_traits <- cliques |>
  group_by(hog) |>
  slice_min(mean_q, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  pivot_longer(all_of(species), names_to = "sp", values_to = "gene") |>
  filter(!is.na(gene)) |>
  summarise(
    n_annual = sum(trait_vec[sp] == "annual"),
    n_perennial = sum(trait_vec[sp] == "perennial"),
    .by = hog
  )

result <- left_join(result, hog_traits, by = "hog")

# Show ranked HOGs per category
show_cols <- c("hog", "n_species", "n_annual", "n_perennial",
               "best_mean_q", "stability_class", "n_densities_survived")
show_cols <- intersect(show_cols, names(result))

Note that stability_class is NA for partial and complete cliques – these span both trait groups and are therefore not trait-exclusive. The jackknife stability test asks “does removing species break trait exclusivity?”, which only applies to trait-exclusive cliques (differentiated and trait_specific). For cross-trait cliques, the n_densities_survived column is the relevant robustness measure.

Partial 

show_classification(result, "partial", show_cols,
                    "Partial HOGs (cross-trait cliques)")

Trait-specific 

show_classification(result, "trait_specific", show_cols,
                    "Trait-specific HOGs (one lineage only)")

Differentiated 

show_classification(result, "differentiated", show_cols,
                    "Differentiated HOGs (within-group conservation, cross-group divergence)")

Complete 

show_classification(result, "complete", show_cols,
                    "Complete HOGs (all 8 species)")

Session info 

sessionInfo()

Developed by Martin Paliocha.
